results.content:
EXECUTIVE PITCH: THE LOGOCENTRICITY ADVANTAGE
The bottleneck of the Quantum Era isn’t #qubit count—it’s the massive overhead of error correction. Traditional methods consume most system resources just to maintain coherence.
Logocentricity approaches the problem at the algebraic level.
The Product:
A fully coded, hardware‑agnostic Governance Engine implementing modal, non‑associative corrective dynamics. It runs today in CPU environments and is architected for GPU and quantum integration.
The Math:
The Clayworth Algebra makes error correction a structural property of the computational manifold. Through Modal Descent, errors contract rather than accumulate.
The Status:
- Equity: 100% founder‑owned
- Corporate: Delaware C‑Corp established
- IP: Provisional patent filed [...] - IP: Provisional patent filed
- Tech Maturity: Engine core is built, tested, and ready for integration work; GPU and quantum execution layers are planned but not yet active
Logocentricity is not building a better qubit. It is building the logical immune system for the future of computation. [...] TECHNICAL NOTE: ALGEBRAIC ERROR CORRECTION VIA MODAL DESCENT
Date: March 19, 2026
To: Future Stakeholders & R&D Partners
From: Logocentricity Inc. (Delaware C-Corp)
Subject: Clayworth Algebra & Hardware-Agnostic QEC Integration
1. ARCHITECTURAL OVERVIEW
The #ClayworthAlgebra replaces procedural syndrome extraction with an intrinsic update rule:
Phi(x) = x [+] Delta(x)
Using the 7x7 #IncidenceMatrix of the #FanoPlane, the system treats errors as #InformationalCurvature. The Axiom of #ModalDescent ensures that higher‑order deviations contract naturally, with Delta^n(x) -> 0 for n > 1.
2. KRIPKE SEMANTICS & CURRENT EXECUTION ENVIRONMENT

#anyonfusion #fano #logocentricity #clayworthalgebra [...] Clayworth Algebra and the Projective‑Geometric Logic Gate: Anyon Fusion as a Complete Quadrangle in PG(2,2)
New work from Logocentricity Inc. shows that the four toric‑code anyons 1, e, m, ε = e⊗m embed as a complete quadrangle in the Fano plane PG(2,2).
The three diagonal points are necessarily collinear and act as fusion witnesses, encoding the full Z₂×Z₂ fusion algebra as categorical composition.
“This embedding is structurally forced by the incidence axioms of PG(2,2); no choice of representation is involved.”
“These six collineations correspond precisely to known logical Clifford gates of the toric code, with correctness guaranteed by the incidence axioms rather than by syndrome measurement.” [...] View profile for Paul Clayworth

The Geometry of Self-Correction: Is the Universe an Error-Correcting Code? 🌀
What if fundamental forces aren't separate "inputs" but emergent corrections from a single geometric engine? Through #HopfFibrations (S¹ → S³ → S²) and #ClayworthAlgebra, I model the universe as actively self-stabilizing against nonassociative drift.
Traditional topology sees Hopf fibers as static linked circles. Clayworth dynamics treats them as \\active trajectories\\—Modal Descent pulling twisted configurations back to coherent manifolds.
🔹 \\Force = Topological Curvature\\
🔹 \\Clayworth Dynamics = Stabilization of that curvature\\
Not fine-tuning. Self-correction.

results.title: #mathematics #algebra #calculus #geometry #ai #computerscience #ml #edgecomputing #constraintpropagation #octonions | Paul Clayworth
results.content:
Why My GF(2) Algebra Echoes the Original Motivation Behind Calculus
When Newton and Leibniz co‑discovered calculus, they weren’t trying to refine Euclid, they were trying to 'escape' him.
Euclidean points were too rigid, too isolated, too static. Motion, flow, and change couldn’t be expressed using points that had no internal notion of adjacency. So they introduced new objects:
• Leibniz’s infinitesimals: dx, dy
• Newton’s fluxions: ẋ, ẏ
These weren’t “points.” They were \relations between points\ — structured differences, directional tendencies, the beginnings of a geometry where meaning comes from interaction, not location.
That conceptual move is exactly the one my (patent pending) algebraic engine makes. [...] • My algebra: treat basis elements as relational nodes linked by M
In all three cases, the geometry is not “the set of points.” The geometry is \\the pattern of allowable interactions\\.
That’s why the filtration tower in my system feels like a “flow.” It \is\ a flow — not in ℝ, but in a snapped, GF(2) relational space where propagation is driven by structure rather than magnitude.
The deeper idea is simple:
\\When points stop being isolated, new mathematics becomes possible.\\
That was the birth of calculus. And it’s the philosophical backbone of this algebraic engine.
Since straightness isn’t a primitive in this system, there’s no analogue of a tangent line — local structure emerges from adjacency and interaction, not from linear limits. [...] diagram

View profile for Paul Clayworth [...] Universal Recursion (UR) is a structural framework for understanding how complex systems are generated, organized and persist across scale. It proposes that phenomena in mathematics, physics, biology and computation can be described in terms of recursive processes—simple rules applied repeatedly to produce increasingly complex structure. UR does not replace existing theories, but reframes them within a common generative structure, preserving their validity while clarifying their relationships across scale.

LodgeiT•12K followers

Clayworth demonstrated that the biological code maintains its integrity under evolutionary stress by "moving up one filtration level" in its geometry. The video demonstrates this same systemic integrity in physical space: you can wind 72 nodes in specific Yin/Yang or Quadrature groups, and the math never breaks; it simply creates a higher-order, perfectly self-resolving loop.
Both Clayworth and the video creator are arguing against randomness. They are showing that whether you are looking at the mutations of a yeast cell or the magnetic windings of a coil, the universe operates as a highly specific, mathematically finite constraint engine.

results.title: Connecting Fano Plane to Genetic Code via GF(2)⁶ | Paul Clayworth posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: #Mathematics #AbstractAlgebra #FiniteGeometry #ProjectiveGeometry #ComputationalBiology #CancerResearch #Genomics #Bioinformatics #OncologyResearch #MathematicalBiology #QuantumErrorCorrection #ClayworthAlgebra #Logocentricity [...] trajectory geometry, codon degeneracy as projective equivalence, and a Clayworth Algebra constraint propagation engine for oncogenic cascade modelling. The epistemic status of each claim is clearly distinguished throughout. [...] This technical note explores the mathematical chain connecting the Fano plane PG(2,2) to the 64-codon genetic code via the direct sum GF(2)³ ⊕ GF(2)³ = GF(2)⁶. We verify computationally that the Gray-code structure of synonymous codons is statistically overwhelming (z = 15.88, p < 0.001); that every major oncogenic driver mutation in TP53, KRAS, BRAF, and PIK3CA corresponds to a Hamming-distance-1 edge traversal in the 6-dimensional binary hypercube Q₆; and that the KRAS G12V mutation closes an exact Fano-plane line in GF(2)⁶ involving the His codon CAC — a specific, testable biological prediction. We state the Fano–Hexagram–Codon (FHC) Conjecture, identify its principal obstacle honestly, and propose three research programs: mutational trajectory geometry, codon degeneracy as projective

results.content:
Provable uniqueness for hallucination reduction — a concise technical note from Paul
I developed an algebraic decomposition that makes hallucination in generative models identifiable and stable. Below I summarize the core result, the proof sketch, and the practical implications you can act on today (bearing in mind, patent pending 😉).
Core statement (one line)
My algebra yields a unique linear decomposition of logits v into a grounded component and a hallucination component, v = G(v) + H(v) , where G is an idempotent projector onto the evidence-aligned subspace and H lies in its complement; this structure gives a single fixed-point grounded solution with provable convergence and tight error bounds.
